keyLegislation: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (AML/CTF Act)
keyConsiderations: ASX continuous disclosure obligations require immediate disclosure of any material Bitcoin treasury decision under Listing Rule 3.1
Directors owe fiduciary duties under ss 180-184 of the Corporations Act — the business judgment rule (s 180(2)) requires demonstrable due diligence
Shareholder approval may be required for significant changes in the nature of the company's activities under ASX Listing Rule 11.1
Fair value measurement of Bitcoin at each reporting date under AASB 13 will create P&L volatility
ASIC may scrutinise disclosure and governance around Bitcoin treasury decisions, particularly for companies with retail shareholders
Institutional investors and proxy advisers may have policies on cryptocurrency exposure that affect voting and engagement
Enhanced financial reporting and audit requirements apply — Bitcoin holdings must be disclosed in the notes to financial statements
Market announcement obligations for material acquisitions or disposals of Bitcoin
Related party transaction rules apply if directors or related entities have cryptocurrency interests
Continuous disclosure obligations mean price-sensitive Bitcoin transactions must be disclosed promptly
commonRisks: ASX query or enforcement action for inadequate continuous disclosure of Bitcoin-related matters
Shareholder class action risk if Bitcoin losses are material and disclosure was inadequate
ASIC investigation for breach of directors' duties if the investment decision was not properly governed
Significant P&L volatility from fair value remeasurement, affecting share price and analyst sentiment
Reputational risk with institutional investors who may view Bitcoin as speculative
Proxy adviser opposition at AGM if the Bitcoin strategy is seen as outside core business
Custody and security risk — loss of Bitcoin would be a material event requiring disclosure
Regulatory change risk — new ASIC or ASX requirements for digital asset holdings
Tax complexity including CGT, transfer pricing (for multinational groups), and thin capitalisation considerations
Banking relationship risk — some banks are cautious about crypto-related entities
